Regulator description:
Financial Conduct Authority is the primary financial regulator for United Kingdom, responsible for supervising entities under its remit to promote market integrity, fair treatment of clients, and confidence in the financial system.

It oversees a broad range of financial services activities—authorisation, ongoing supervision, conduct standards, and enforcement actions where needed.

Typical tools include licensing/authorisation requirements, ongoing reporting and audits, rules on client money handling, disclosure standards, and enforcement powers such as fines, restrictions, or licence actions.

The exact protections available to a retail trader (compensation schemes, leverage caps, negative balance protection, etc.) depend on the regulated entity the client is onboarded to and the products offered under that licence.

Retail leverage restrictions (typical): UK CFD caps aligned to ESMA-style limits (retail)
Notes: PRA is prudential; include for completeness.

It oversees a broad range of financial services activities—authorisation, ongoing supervision, conduct standards, and enforcement actions where needed.

Typical tools include licensing/authorisation requirements, ongoing reporting and audits, rules on client money handling, disclosure standards, and enforcement powers such as fines, restrictions, or licence actions.

The exact protections available to a retail trader (compensation schemes, leverage caps, negative balance protection, etc.) depend on the regulated entity the client is onboarded to and the products offered under that licence.

Retail leverage restrictions (typical): ESMA-style CFD caps: 30:1 FX majors; 20:1 minors/gold; 10:1 commodities; 5:1 equities; 2:1 crypto (retail)

It oversees a broad range of financial services activities—authorisation, ongoing supervision, conduct standards, and enforcement actions where needed.

Typical tools include licensing/authorisation requirements, ongoing reporting and audits, rules on client money handling, disclosure standards, and enforcement powers such as fines, restrictions, or licence actions.

The exact protections available to a retail trader (compensation schemes, leverage caps, negative balance protection, etc.) depend on the regulated entity the client is onboarded to and the products offered under that licence.

Compensation limits: Varies by country (commonly up to €20,000 under EEA ICS)
Retail leverage restrictions (typical): ESMA-style CFD caps: 30:1 FX majors; 20:1 minors/gold; 10:1 commodities; 5:1 equities; 2:1 crypto (retail)
Notes: Use country-specific ICS values if needed.
